title: Running sshd inside container produce wired error due to pam_loginuid.
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

I have a container running as a SSH jump host on a remote server. Recently I'm trying to switch from docker to podman. And then, argh, podman breaks my jump host in a very weird way: the SSH authentication step finished successfully, but my SSH client failed with an error code 254.

I took some time investigating the details. Later it turns out that the SSH client failed because it cannot complete the pam session on the remote server. More specifically, it failed to run a pam module called pam_loginuid. I searched on the Internet, and noticed that it seems to be a bug that has been already fixed by docker. 

Reference: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/21391142/why-is-it-needed-to-set-pam-loginuid-to-its-optional-value-with-docker

Any ideas?

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Run an OpenSSH server (v7.4 in my case) instead a container. Make sure the following line is in its PAM config:
```
session    required     pam_loginuid.so
```

2. Run an OpenSSH client (v7.9 in my case) to connect the server.

**Describe the results you received:**

The client exited with error code 254.

**Describe the results you expected:**

The client connect to the server successfully.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:            1.4.3
RemoteAPI Version:  1
Go Version:         go1.10.4
OS/Arch:            linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```
debug:
  compiler: gc
  git commit: ""
  go version: go1.10.4
  podman version: 1.4.3
host:
  BuildahVersion: 1.9.0
  Conmon:
    package: 'conmon: /usr/libexec/podman/conmon'
    path: /usr/libexec/podman/conmon
    version: 'conmon version 1.0.0-rc2, commit: unknown'
  Distribution:
    distribution: ubuntu
    version: "19.04"
  MemFree: 1875042304
  MemTotal: 8361558016
  OCIRuntime:
    package: 'cri-o-runc: /usr/lib/cri-o-runc/sbin/runc'
    path: /usr/lib/cri-o-runc/sbin/runc
    version: 'runc version spec: 1.0.1-dev'
  SwapFree: 2145382400
  SwapTotal: 2147479552
  arch: amd64
  cpus: 4
  hostname: emerson-VirtualBox
  kernel: 5.0.0-16-generic
  os: linux
  rootless: true
  uptime: 3h 41m 20.5s (Approximately 0.12 days)
registries:
  blocked: null
  insecure: null
  search: null
store:
  ConfigFile: /home/emerson/.config/containers/storage.conf
  ContainerStore:
    number: 0
  GraphDriverName: vfs
  GraphOptions: null
  GraphRoot: /home/emerson/.local/share/containers/storage
  GraphStatus: {}
  ImageStore:
    number: 0
  RunRoot: /run/user/1000
  VolumePath: /home/emerson/.local/share/containers/storage/volumes
```

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**
The test output pasted here is from a VirtualBox instance, but a bare-metal machine can produce the same error.

comments.body:
I do not think that we want to add `CAP_AUDIT_WRITE` to the default container capabilities. Maintaining a minimal set of capabilities improves the security of containers, and most containers will not require AUDIT_WRITE for normal operation. If you want to use it, you can add `--cap-add audit_write` to your Podman command line.

You can also look into making the loginuid module optional - the linked StackOverflow article notes that this ought to be possible.
Ummm, cool, if that's about security concerns, then I'm totally okay with it. I'll close the ticket and consider using optional pam module instead. Thanks for your reply.

@mheon, Podman is currently setting AUDIT_WRITE
```
[libpod (master)]$ podman top -l capeff
EFFECTIVE CAPS
AUDIT_WRITE,CHOWN,DAC_OVERRIDE,FOWNER,FSETID,KILL,MKNOD,NET_BIND_SERVICE,NET_RAW,SETFCAP,SETGID,SETPCAP,SETUID,SYS_CHROOT
```
Huh. Guess it must have made its way into the default caps in the OCI generator. I had a feeling we wouldn't want it because it can be used to break our `loginuid` chain.
Personally I think we should drop it.  Running sshd inside of a container should not be supported by default, users should have to do a --cap-add AUDIT_WRITE if they want this to work.
Is your failure SELinux or trying to do this as rootless?  I would figure rootless would not work.

@mheon After reading @vrothberg 's reply, I got curious and tested SSHD in container with the `--cap-add CAP_AUDIT_WRITE` flag. Surprisingly, that didn't fix the problem. There's something else breaks pam_loginuid.so in podman.
Interesting. Can you provide a few more details to help reproduce? Podman command used to run the container, whether you're running as root?

@mheon @rhatdan I'm running podman as root, with SELinux disabled. The podman command I ran unfortunately requires a private container image that I cannot provide. But I'll try find a valid public image to help reproduce the problem.

@mheon __Reproduce Steps:__
1. Add 'docker.io' to podman's registries.
2. Start a Fedora 20 container using
``` bash
sudo podman run -it fedora:20 /bin/bash
```
3. Install OpenSSH server using
``` bash
yum -y install openssh-server
ssh-keygen -t rsa -f /etc/ssh/ssh_host_rsa_key -N ""
ssh-keygen -t dsa -f /etc/ssh/ssh_host_dsa_key -N ""
ssh-keygen -t ecdsa -f /etc/ssh/ssh_host_ecdsa_key -N ""
```
4. Create a temporary user and set its password.
5. Start SSHD on port 2222 with debug output
``` bash
/usr/sbin/sshd -d -p 2222
```
6. SSH to the container from the outside
``` bash
ssh -p 2222 <container IP>
```
fedora 20?

using your instructions and the latest master build, i was able to make this work perfectly.  the key is to use the latest **fedora** package that is actually from **fedora**.

```
sudo podman run -it --rm registry.fedoraproject.org/fedora:latest /bin/bash
[root@24899cac9bdd /]# dnf -y install passwd openssh-server
...
[root@24899cac9bdd /]# ssh-keygen -t rsa -f /etc/ssh/ssh_host_rsa_key -N ""
...
[root@24899cac9bdd /]# ssh-keygen -t dsa -f /etc/ssh/ssh_host_dsa_key -N ""
...
[root@24899cac9bdd /]# ssh-keygen -t ecdsa -f /etc/ssh/ssh_host_ecdsa_key -N ""
...
[root@24899cac9bdd /]# useradd foobar
[root@24899cac9bdd /]# passwd foobar
...
[root@24899cac9bdd /]# /usr/sbin/sshd -d -p 2222
...
debug1: Bind to port 2222 on ::.
Server listening on :: port 2222.
```

In another terminal, ssh into the container.

Cool, thanks @baude , that's a really helpful observation for me.

Another interesting fact is, I just tested both Fedora 20 and latest Fedora on docker 19.03.1, sshd is working properly on both of them. So the old Fedora must have something inside its pam_loginuid that's working for docker, but won't work in podman. Is this fact helpful for you guys?

By the way, I feel I may have to briefly explain why I choose Fedora 20 to reproduce this bug.

I choose Fedora 20 because this bug was found on a RHEL 7.5 container (which is the majority of the RHEL systems I guess). I'm not familiar with the version mapping between Fedora and RHEL, but I roughly remember that some old RHEL 7 releases was based on Fedora 19~20. That's why I pick Fedora 20. Moreover, that means this bug may also exists in some newer Fedora versions.

@rhatdan @mheon ring any bells?

@Gh0u1L5 lemme try this on a centos container, much closer imho

The following works as expected.  Please try building this Dockerfile.
```
FROM registry.centos.org/centos:latest
RUN yum -y update && yum -y install openssh-server passwd && yum clean all
RUN ssh-keygen -t rsa -f /etc/ssh/ssh_host_rsa_key -N "" && ssh-keygen -t dsa -f /etc/ssh/ssh_host_dsa_key -N "" && ssh-keygen -t ecdsa -f /etc/ssh/ssh_host_ecdsa_key -N ""
RUN useradd foobar && echo "foobar:foobar" | chpasswd
RUN sed 's@session\s*required\s*pam_loginuid.so@session optional pam_loginuid.so@g' -i /etc/pam.d/sshd
```

Also, reference: https://docs.docker.com/engine/examples/running_ssh_service/
@Gh0u1L5 I hope my feedback works ... given that Fedora 20 is way past end-of-life, it is a tough scenario to work with.

One key difference between podman and docker is that when you run podman the  /proc/self/loginuid would be set, not sure if this would cause issues.   Since pam_loginuid will fail when it tries to set it.  In Docker it will not be set.
```
# podman run fedora cat /proc/self/loginuid 
3267
 ```
That is my UID.

```
# docker run fedora cat /proc/self/loginuid 
4294967295
```
That is the UID=-1 basically meaning the docker daemon was never started by a logged in user.

I would also try to run the sshd container fully privileged to see if something else is causing an issue.
may we close?
No, we're still working this one
This issue had no activity for 30 days. In the absence of activity or the "do-not-close" label, the issue will be automatically closed within 7 days.
I think this is fixed.  I don't see this as a bug.  It is just a configuration issue in the container image as @baude pointed out.  Reopen if you disagree.

@rhatdan, I kind of disagree with this being a configuration issue of the container.  That change of `pam_loginuid.so` from required to optional is a workaround.  

However I wonder if that workaround could impose a security issue in some way?  Anyways, the root cause should really be fixed instead.

Unless I have totally misunderstood something, then this bug has been reported in Debian ([726661](https://bugs.debian.org/cgi-bin/bugreport.cgi?bug=726661), OCT 2013), LXC ([#661](https://github.com/lxc/lxc/issues/661), SEP 2015).  

One of the Ubuntu/LXC developpers made a patch for this issue in linux-pam (https://github.com/linux-pam/linux-pam/commit/2e62d5aea3f5ac267cfa54f0ea1f8c07ac85a95a) JAN 2014.

[BZ#1728777](https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=1728777) was opened JUN 2019, regarding this patch nog being present in RH, and it seems to have been acceptet (Status: ON_QA → VERIFIED).

So i agree, this is not a bug in podman, it rather seems like a bug in RH and thus also CentOs, and potentially other distros as well, if they aren't using a "newer" version of pam or have backported that patch.

Sorry to bring up a closed ticket like this, but I felt like it was missing some background info, for future people coming by with this issue (like myself).
